behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA applies: correctness depends on real OS filesystem watcher behavior (recursive/subdirectory event delivery) and proving an externally visible side effect (output produced). Unit-only and pure in-process fakes can miss watcher/runtime differences. Rejected Integration because behavior text requires actual watch semantics for subdirectories.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temporary directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file there, and verify via round-trip readback that exactly one corresponding output artifact appears in the output directory.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS is low for this specific behavior: it is primarily deterministic path+time-window logic and does not inherently require OS/network/subprocess or model output. Rejected higher levels because behavior text is explicitly about deduplication logic, not filesystem event peculiarities.
behaviors.test_description: Exercise the dedup function/object with controlled timestamps and config window values, assert first request is accepted and second same-path request inside window is rejected, then accepted again after window expiry.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on real OS event emission patterns, so REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both trigger System. In-process mocks can force synthetic duplicates but may not reproduce actual watcher burst/coalescing behavior.
behaviors.test_description: With the daemon running on a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple native FS events, then verify exactly one processing/output result for that file path.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is OS/filesystem lifecycle behavior (delete/recreate, watcher reattachment) and requires proving externally visible recovery while process stays alive. Integration fakes can miss real watcher invalidation/re-subscription behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon, delete watched directory, recreate it at same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Although ordering logic can be unit-tested, this behavior ties to real file arrival concurrency and filesystem timestamp semantics/precision, which are infrastructure-dependent. Rejected Integration because behavior text anchors order to file creation timestamps from the filesystem boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in a real watched directory with controlled timestamp ordering, run daemon, and verify output/processing log order matches creation timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real write-in-progress behavior, event timing, and file readiness at OS boundary; mocks often hide these races. System is needed to validate the round-trip effect that processing starts only after complete write.
behaviors.test_description: In a real watched directory, write a file in chunks with delays while daemon runs; assert no processing/output appears before final chunk/close, then assert processing occurs after completion.
